title: Acute Kidney Injury
oneLiner: Acute decline in kidney function (hours–days) causing waste accumulation and fluid/electrolyte derangement; affects up to 20% of hospitalised patients; early recognition saves lives.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
- **Classic vignette:** A 72-year-old man with type 2 diabetes, hypertension and recent diarrhoea is admitted after being found confused at home. He is oliguric (<200 ml/24 h), his JVP is flat, mucous membranes dry, creatinine is 350 µmol/L (baseline 90), potassium 6.1 mmol/L, and urine dip shows no blood/protein. He is on ramipril and metformin.

- **Dissect the vignette:**
  - *Recent diarrhoea + flat JVP + dry mucosa* → hypovolaemia (pre-renal trigger)
  - *Oliguria* → severe reduction in GFR (<10 ml/min if <0.5 ml/kg/h)
  - *Acute rise in creatinine: 90→350* → AKI stage 3 (3× baseline)
  - *Hyperkalaemia (K+ 6.1)* → common in oliguric AKI; risk of cardiac arrest
  - *On ramipril* → ACE inhibitor reduces efferent arteriolar tone, worsening GFR in hypovolaemia
  - *On metformin* → risk of lactic acidosis in AKI (stop immediately)
  - *No blood/protein on dip* → points toward pre-renal or acute tubular necrosis (ATN), not glomerulonephritis (GN)

- **Atypical presentations that may trick you:**
  1. **Non-oliguric AKI** – e.g. aminoglycoside toxicity; output may be normal or high, but creatinine rises. Missed if you only monitor urine output.
  2. **Rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis (RPGN)** – presents with AKI but also haematuria, red cell casts, hypertension, oedema. Often diagnosed late.
  3. **Post-renal AKI** – initially anuria then polyuria if obstruction partially relieved; patient may deny pain. Renal colic absent if chronic obstruction (e.g. pelvic malignancy). Always do a bladder scan.
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
- **Plain-language definition:** AKI is a sudden (hours–days) drop in the kidneys’ ability to filter waste, balance fluids and regulate electrolytes. It is diagnosed by a rise in creatinine, fall in urine output, or both.

- **Why this topic matters:**
  - **Frequency:** 20% of hospitalised patients, 50% in ICU. It is a common trigger for in-hospital deterioration.
  - **Stakes:** Hyperkalaemia and pulmonary oedema can kill in minutes. Even stage 1 AKI increases short-term mortality by 30–60%.
  - **Common pitfalls:** The silent nature means you can miss it if you don’t check baseline creatinine; ordering the right tests early (e.g. urine electrolytes, urine sediment) can differentiate cause and save the kidney.
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
- **The story:** AKI is a failure of glomerular filtration – either because blood flow to the kidney is low (pre-renal), the kidney tissue itself is damaged (intrinsic), or urine outflow is blocked (post-renal). All three reduce GFR, leading to accumulation of creatinine, urea and potassium.

- **Mechanism to sign:**
  - *Hypoperfusion (pre-renal)* → decreased GFR → reduced urine output (oliguria) → body holds onto Na and water → urine becomes concentrated (low urine Na, FENa<1%). To maintain pressure, RAAS is activated → higher BP in the glomerulus → but if hypovolaemia persists, tubular cells become ischaemic → injury (pre-renal → ATN).
  - *Tubular cell death (ATN)* → sloughing of cells forms “muddy brown casts” in urine. Loss of concentrating ability → urine osmolality <350; impaired Na reabsorption → FENa>2%. Necrosis releases intracellular contents: K+ leak → hyperkalaemia; acidosis from reduced H+ secretion.
  - *Obstruction (post-renal)* → increased back-pressure causes tubular dilatation, compression of peritubular capillaries → reduced GFR → anuria then oliguria. Bladder distension → suprapubic pain. Ultrasound shows hydronephrosis.
  - *Hyperkalaemia* mechanism: reduced K+ excretion in the distal tubule → K+ rises. ECG changes (peaked T, wide QRS) indicate impending arrest.
  - *Metabolic acidosis*: kidneys cannot regenerate bicarbonate or excrete H+ → serum pH drops, K+ shifts out of cells (aggravates hyperkalaemia).

- **Connecting pattern recognition:** In the vignette, diarrhoea caused volume depletion → pre-renal state ± ACE inhibitor → critical reduction in GFR → oliguria, K+ rise, acidosis, confusion (uraemia).
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
| Category | Cause | Stem Clue |
|----------|-------|-----------|
| **Pre-renal (most common)** | Hypovolaemia (diarrhoea, burns, bleeding, over-diuresis) | “Several days of watery stools” or “burns 20% BSA” |
| | Reduced effective circulating volume (cardiac failure, cirrhosis, sepsis) | “Distended neck veins and pedal oedema” but low urine output |
| | Renal artery stenosis + ACEi/ARB | “Sudden AKI after starting ramipril, bilateral renal artery stenosis” |
| | NSAIDs (inhibit afferent vasodilation) | “On ibuprofen to treat knee pain” |
| **Intrinsic – Tubular** | Ischaemic ATN (prolonged pre-renal) | “Hypotensive episode during surgery” |
| | Nephrotoxins (aminoglycosides, contrast, cisplatin, myoglobin) | “Recently received gentamicin” or “Crush injury” (rhabdomyolysis) |
| | Pigment nephropathy (haemoglobin/myoglobin) | “Dark urine, positive blood on dip but no RBCs” |
| **Intrinsic – Interstitial** | Acute interstitial nephritis (AIN): antibiotics (penicillins, cephalosporins, rifampicin), NSAIDs, PPI, allopurinol | “Fever, rash, eosinophilia, sterile pyuria” |
| **Intrinsic – Glomerular** | Acute glomerulonephritis (post-streptococcal, IgA, RPGN) | “Red-cell casts, proteinuria, hypertension, oedema” |
| | Vasculitis (ANCA-associated, Goodpasture’s) | “Pulmonary haemorrhage, haemoptysis, crescents on biopsy” |
| **Post-renal** | Prostatic hypertrophy, pelvic cancer, stones, retroperitoneal fibrosis | “Sudden anuria” or “palpable bladder and hydronephrosis” |

> **Most common cause:** Pre-renal (volume depletion) in ward patients; in ICU, sepsis is the largest contributor, often mixed pre-renal + ATN.
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
- **Diagnostic criteria (KDIGO 2012):** Increase in creatinine ≥26.5 µmol/L (0.3 mg/dL) within 48 h, or ≥1.5× baseline within 7 days, or urine output <0.5 ml/kg/h for 6 hours (Stage 1). Staging: 1–3 based on magnitude of creatinine rise or duration of oliguria.

- **Test interpretation pattern-recognition:**

| Test | What it shows | Why order | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|-----------|----------------|
| Serum creatinine | GFR surrogate | Baseline + serial trend | Rise >26.5 µmol/L in 48 h = AKI. Doubling = Stage 2. Tripling = Stage 3 |
| Urine output | GFR (if diuretics not given) | Hourly in ICU, 4–6 h on ward | <0.5 ml/kg/h = oliguric; <0.3 ml/kg/h = severe |
| Urine dipstick | Blood, protein, leucocytes, nitrites | Screen for GN, infection, myoglobinuria | Haematuria + proteinuria → suspect GN. Blood + no RBCs → myoglobin/haemoglobin |
| Urine sediment microscopy | Casts, cells | Differentiate ATN, GN, AIN | Muddy brown granular casts = ATN; RBC casts = GN; WBC casts = AIN |
| Urine Na, creatinine, osmolality (FENa) | Tubular handling | Pre-renal vs intrinsic | FENa <1% = pre-renal; >2% = intrinsic (ATN). Note: diuretics invalidate FENa – use urea instead (FEUrea <35% pre-renal) |
| Serum K+, HCO3-, pH, Cl- | Electrolyte and acid-base | Assess hyperkalaemia, acidosis, anion gap | HyperK+ (need ECG). Metabolic acidosis with high anion gap = uraemia or lactic acidosis |
| Renal ultrasound | Hydronephrosis, kidney size, stones | Rule out obstruction (post-renal) | Hydronephrosis + ureteric dilatation = obstruction. Small kidneys (<9 cm) suggest chronic disease |
| Urine eosinophils | Eosinophiluria | Suspect AIN | Positive (but insensitive) – more useful is clinical picture + fever/rash/eosinophilia |

- **Single finding that confirms:**
  - **Pre-renal:** FENa <1% and urine osmol >500 (in the absence of diuretics)
  - **ATN:** Muddy brown granular casts + FENa >2%
  - **Post-renal:** Hydronephrosis on ultrasound
  - **GN:** Red cell casts on microscopy
  - **AIN:** WBC casts + eosinophiluria + drug exposure
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
- **Immediate (first hour)**
  - **Stop nephrotoxins:** ACEi, ARB, NSAIDs, diuretics, metformin, aminoglycosides, contrast
  - **Assess volume status:** JVP, BP, skin turgor, lung auscultation for crackles (fluid overload)
    - *If hypovolaemic:* Give 500 ml bolus of 0.9% saline over 15 min; reassess. Aim for urine output >5 ml/kg/h over the next hour. Use Hartmann’s if suspect non-anion gap acidosis.
    - *If hypervolaemic:* Restrict fluids; loop diuretic challenge (furosemide 40 mg IV; if no response, escalate).
  - **Treat hyperkalaemia (K+ >6.0 or ECG changes):**
    - Cardiac protection: 10 ml of 10% calcium gluconate IV over 2–3 min (repeats if ECG remains abnormal)
    - Shift K+ into cells: 10 units Actrapid + 50 ml 50% dextrose IV over 5 min (onset 15 min, peak 30–60 min)
    - Alternatively: 10 mg salbutamol nebulised (Caution: tachycardia, can cause hypokalaemia)
    - Remove K+: consider oral/rectal calcium resonium 15 g q8h (slow), or haemodialysis if refractory
  - **Correct acidosis:** If pH <7.2 and HCO3- <16, give 1.26% sodium bicarbonate 250 ml IV over 30 min (Caution: may precipitate hypocalcaemia).
  - **Treat precipitant:** If sepsis – start antibiotics within 1 hour; if urinary obstruction – pass a urinary catheter.

- **Ward (24–72 hours)**
  - **Monitor:** Strict fluid balance, urine output, daily creatinine, K+, Ca2+, PO4-, HCO3-, Hb, lactate
  - **Fluids:** Maintain euvolaemia. If still oliguric, consider furosemide 40–80 mg IV once (not proven to alter renal outcome but useful to avoid overload). Do not routinely give dopamine or mannitol (no benefit).
  - **Drug dose adjustment:** Use eGFR to dose all renally cleared drugs (e.g. antibiotics). Check BNF / local guidelines.
  - **Nutrition:** Restrict K+ (<2 g/day), PO4 (<800 mg/day), Na+ (<2 g/day). Ensure adequate calories (20–30 kcal/kg/day) – consider NG feeding if oral intake poor.
  - **Avoid:** IV contrast unless essential, NSAIDs, potassium-sparing diuretics, and high-protein diets.

- **Long-term**
  - **Renal replacement therapy (RRT) – indications (“AEIOU”):** A: severe acidosis (pH <7.2); E: electrolyte imbalance (K+ >6.5 refractory); I: Intoxication (e.g. lithium, methanol); O: Overload (pulmonary oedema not responsive to diuretics); U: Uraemic symptoms (encephalopathy, pericarditis, bleeding). Start haemodialysis (or CRRT in unstable patients).
  - **Follow-up:** All AKI patients need creatinine check at 3 months to assess recovery. Residual CKD is common. Avoid nephrotoxins lifelong.
  - **Education:** Patients should stay well hydrated, stop NSAIDs, and know what to do if vomiting/diarrhoea.
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | AKI (oliguric) | Pre-renal Azotaemia | ATN (Ischaemic) | AIN | Post-renal Obstruction |
|---------|----------------|---------------------|-----------------|-----|-------------------------|
| **Onset** | Rapid (hours–days) | Rapid, with volume loss | 1–3 days after insult | Days after drug start | Gradual or sudden |
| **Pain** | May have flank pain if renal capsule stretches | None | None | Flank pain ± fever | Colicky if stone; dull if chronic |
| **Urine output** | Oliguria (<0.5 ml/kg/h) | Oliguria | Oliguria (but non-oliguric possible) | Oliguria or polyuria | Anuria / polyuria (alternating) |
| **Urine sediment** | Variable; find casts | Few hyaline casts | Muddy brown granular casts | WBC casts, eosinophils (sterile pyuria) | Normal or WBCs if infection |
| **FENa** | Variable | <1% | >2% | Variable (often >1%) | Variable |
| **BUN:Cr ratio** | 10–15:1 | >20:1 (high BUN) | <15:1 | <15:1 | Variable |
| **Blood** | HyperK+, acidosis, uraemia | High BUN, Cr mildly elevated | HyperK+, acidosis, elevated Cr | Eosinophilia (if drug-induced) | Cr rises slowly, may be asymmetric |
| **Imaging** | Normal (unless chronic) | Normal | Normal | Normal or enlarged kidney | Hydronephrosis, dilated ureter, bladder distension |
| **Key differentiator** | Look for cause | Volume history + low urine Na + high BUN:Cr | Recent hypotension/toxins + FENa >2% | Drug exposure + eosinophilia + sterile pyuria | Ultrasound: hydronephrosis + post-void residual |
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
- **Hyperkalaemia (>6.5 mmol/L):** Peaked T waves, loss of P wave, wide QRS → VF/asystole. *Action:* Calcium gluconate, insulin+dextrose, bicarbonate; emergency dialysis if refractory.
- **Pulmonary oedema:** Patients become dyspnoeic, crackles up to apices, CXR shows batwing shadows; low oxygen sats. *Action:* high-flow O2, IV nitrates (GTN 10–200 µg/min), non-invasive ventilation; if no response – urgent ultrafiltration (dialysis or haemofiltration).
- **Uraemic pericarditis:** Chest pain, fever, pericardial rub, diffuse ST elevation on ECG. *Late sign:* If untreated can cause tamponade. *Action:* urgent dialysis, NSAIDs/colchicine (but risk of bleeding).
- **Uraemic encephalopathy:** Lethargy, confusion, asterixis, myoclonus, seizures, coma. *Action:* dialysis (IV thiamine if suspicion of Wernicke’s).
- **Metabolic acidosis:** Serum HCO3- <15, pH <7.30, compensatory hyperventilation (Kussmaul breathing). *Action:* correct with bicarbonate if severe; dialysis if refractory.
- **Anaemia of CKD:** Normocytic, multifactorial (erythropoietin deficiency, uraemic inhibitors). *Action:* epoetin alfa (start when Hb <100 g/L) – but not acutely.
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
- **Mnemonics:**
  - **HAS PINK** – Pre-renal causes: *H*ypovolaemia, *A*CEi/ARB, *S*epsis, *P*ancreatitis, *I*leus, *N*SAIDs, *K*idney artery stenosis.
  - **AEIOU** – Indications for dialysis: *A*cidosis (refractory), *E*lectrolyte (K+ >6.5), *I*ntoxication, *O*verload (pulmonary oedema), *U*raemic symptoms.
  - **CRASH** – Consequences of uraemia: *C*onvulsions, *R*etinitis (or asterixis), *A*cidosis, *S*erous membrane inflammation (pericarditis), *H*yperkalaemia.

- **Clinical pearls:**
  1. **BUN:Cr ratio >20:1** is highly suggestive of pre-renal azotaemia (but not reliable if patient has GI bleeding or high protein catabolism).
  2. **Urine dip positive for blood but no RBCs on microscopy = myoglobinuria** (rhabdomyolysis). Check CK; treat with aggressive IV fluids ± sodium bicarbonate.
  3. **Non-oliguric AKI (normal urine output)** is easier to manage (fluid balance) and has better prognosis. Always check creatinine even if output is normal.
  4. **Before starting diuretics in AKI, ensure the patient is euvolemic/hypervolemic.** Diuretics in hypovolaemia worsen injury.
  5. **Contrast-induced nephropathy** – risk highest in CKD, diabetes, volume depletion, high contrast volume (>300 ml). Hydrate with 0.9% saline 1 ml/kg/h for 6–12 h before and after; withhold metformin 48 h.
  6. **Sepsis is the most common cause of AKI in ICU.** Early source control, adequate fluid resuscitation (but avoid fluid overload), and antimicrobial therapy.
  7. **Always check a drug history.** Common culprits: NSAIDs, ACEi/ARB, PPIs, ciprofloxacin, rifampicin, proton pump inhibitors (AIN), and over-the-counter “herbal” remedies (e.g. Chinese herbs).

- **Common exam/ward mistakes to avoid:**
  - Not measuring urine output (must be charted).
  - Giving potassium supplements without checking K+.
  - Assuming oliguria is due to “dehydration” without assessing volume status.
  - Using urinary catheter as the first step for oliguria (can cause infection); first do bladder scan.
  - Starting dialysis too late (especially in hyperkalaemia with ECG changes).
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
- **Call your registrar/senior immediately if:**
  - Creatinine rises >50% in 24 h, or >100% in 48 h (Stage 3)
  - Urine output <0.3 ml/kg/h for >6 h (Stage 3)
  - Hyperkalaemia >6.0 mmol/L not responding to initial treatment (or any ECG changes)
  - pH <7.2 (or HCO3- <12) despite bicarbonate therapy
  - Pulmonary oedema (respiratory rate >30, SpO2 <90% on 15 L O2, crackles, CXR edema)
  - GCS <12 or new neurological signs (uraemic encephalopathy)
  - NEWS2 ≥7 or lactate >4 mmol/L

- **When to discuss ICU / critical care:**
  - Any need for renal replacement therapy (dialysis)
  - Ongoing haemodynamic instability (MAP <65 despite fluids, on vasopressors)
  - Severe metabolic acidosis (pH <7.1) not correctable
  - Multiorgan failure (e.g. AKI + respiratory failure requiring ventilation)

- **When to call the crash team:**
  - Cardiac arrest (VF/asystole from hyperkalaemia)
  - Unresponsive, moribund patient with gross fluid overload and impending respiratory arrest

> **Bottom line for FY1:** A patient with AKI can deteriorate fast. Your job is to identify the cause (pre-renal, renal, post-renal), stop the trigger, treat hyperkalaemia, restore volume, and escalate early. Every hour counts.

rawMarkdown:
# Acute Kidney Injury

**One-liner:** Acute decline in kidney function (hours–days) causing waste accumulation and fluid/electrolyte derangement; affects up to 20% of hospitalised patients; early recognition saves lives.

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

- **Classic vignette:** A 72-year-old man with type 2 diabetes, hypertension and recent diarrhoea is admitted after being found confused at home. He is oliguric (<200 ml/24 h), his JVP is flat, mucous membranes dry, creatinine is 350 µmol/L (baseline 90), potassium 6.1 mmol/L, and urine dip shows no blood/protein. He is on ramipril and metformin.

- **Dissect the vignette:**
  - *Recent diarrhoea + flat JVP + dry mucosa* → hypovolaemia (pre-renal trigger)
  - *Oliguria* → severe reduction in GFR (<10 ml/min if <0.5 ml/kg/h)
  - *Acute rise in creatinine: 90→350* → AKI stage 3 (3× baseline)
  - *Hyperkalaemia (K+ 6.1)* → common in oliguric AKI; risk of cardiac arrest
  - *On ramipril* → ACE inhibitor reduces efferent arteriolar tone, worsening GFR in hypovolaemia
  - *On metformin* → risk of lactic acidosis in AKI (stop immediately)
  - *No blood/protein on dip* → points toward pre-renal or acute tubular necrosis (ATN), not glomerulonephritis (GN)

- **Atypical presentations that may trick you:**
  1. **Non-oliguric AKI** – e.g. aminoglycoside toxicity; output may be normal or high, but creatinine rises. Missed if you only monitor urine output.
  2. **Rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis (RPGN)** – presents with AKI but also haematuria, red cell casts, hypertension, oedema. Often diagnosed late.
  3. **Post-renal AKI** – initially anuria then polyuria if obstruction partially relieved; patient may deny pain. Renal colic absent if chronic obstruction (e.g. pelvic malignancy). Always do a bladder scan.

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

- **Plain-language definition:** AKI is a sudden (hours–days) drop in the kidneys’ ability to filter waste, balance fluids and regulate electrolytes. It is diagnosed by a rise in creatinine, fall in urine output, or both.

- **Why this topic matters:**
  - **Frequency:** 20% of hospitalised patients, 50% in ICU. It is a common trigger for in-hospital deterioration.
  - **Stakes:** Hyperkalaemia and pulmonary oedema can kill in minutes. Even stage 1 AKI increases short-term mortality by 30–60%.
  - **Common pitfalls:** The silent nature means you can miss it if you don’t check baseline creatinine; ordering the right tests early (e.g. urine electrolytes, urine sediment) can differentiate cause and save the kidney.

### 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings

- **The story:** AKI is a failure of glomerular filtration – either because blood flow to the kidney is low (pre-renal), the kidney tissue itself is damaged (intrinsic), or urine outflow is blocked (post-renal). All three reduce GFR, leading to accumulation of creatinine, urea and potassium.

- **Mechanism to sign:**
  - *Hypoperfusion (pre-renal)* → decreased GFR → reduced urine output (oliguria) → body holds onto Na and water → urine becomes concentrated (low urine Na, FENa<1%). To maintain pressure, RAAS is activated → higher BP in the glomerulus → but if hypovolaemia persists, tubular cells become ischaemic → injury (pre-renal → ATN).
  - *Tubular cell death (ATN)* → sloughing of cells forms “muddy brown casts” in urine. Loss of concentrating ability → urine osmolality <350; impaired Na reabsorption → FENa>2%. Necrosis releases intracellular contents: K+ leak → hyperkalaemia; acidosis from reduced H+ secretion.
  - *Obstruction (post-renal)* → increased back-pressure causes tubular dilatation, compression of peritubular capillaries → reduced GFR → anuria then oliguria. Bladder distension → suprapubic pain. Ultrasound shows hydronephrosis.
  - *Hyperkalaemia* mechanism: reduced K+ excretion in the distal tubule → K+ rises. ECG changes (peaked T, wide QRS) indicate impending arrest.
  - *Metabolic acidosis*: kidneys cannot regenerate bicarbonate or excrete H+ → serum pH drops, K+ shifts out of cells (aggravates hyperkalaemia).

- **Connecting pattern recognition:** In the vignette, diarrhoea caused volume depletion → pre-renal state ± ACE inhibitor → critical reduction in GFR → oliguria, K+ rise, acidosis, confusion (uraemia).

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)

| Category | Cause | Stem Clue |
|----------|-------|-----------|
| **Pre-renal (most common)** | Hypovolaemia (diarrhoea, burns, bleeding, over-diuresis) | “Several days of watery stools” or “burns 20% BSA” |
| | Reduced effective circulating volume (cardiac failure, cirrhosis, sepsis) | “Distended neck veins and pedal oedema” but low urine output |
| | Renal artery stenosis + ACEi/ARB | “Sudden AKI after starting ramipril, bilateral renal artery stenosis” |
| | NSAIDs (inhibit afferent vasodilation) | “On ibuprofen to treat knee pain” |
| **Intrinsic – Tubular** | Ischaemic ATN (prolonged pre-renal) | “Hypotensive episode during surgery” |
| | Nephrotoxins (aminoglycosides, contrast, cisplatin, myoglobin) | “Recently received gentamicin” or “Crush injury” (rhabdomyolysis) |
| | Pigment nephropathy (haemoglobin/myoglobin) | “Dark urine, positive blood on dip but no RBCs” |
| **Intrinsic – Interstitial** | Acute interstitial nephritis (AIN): antibiotics (penicillins, cephalosporins, rifampicin), NSAIDs, PPI, allopurinol | “Fever, rash, eosinophilia, sterile pyuria” |
| **Intrinsic – Glomerular** | Acute glomerulonephritis (post-streptococcal, IgA, RPGN) | “Red-cell casts, proteinuria, hypertension, oedema” |
| | Vasculitis (ANCA-associated, Goodpasture’s) | “Pulmonary haemorrhage, haemoptysis, crescents on biopsy” |
| **Post-renal** | Prostatic hypertrophy, pelvic cancer, stones, retroperitoneal fibrosis | “Sudden anuria” or “palpable bladder and hydronephrosis” |

> **Most common cause:** Pre-renal (volume depletion) in ward patients; in ICU, sepsis is the largest contributor, often mixed pre-renal + ATN.

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

- **Diagnostic criteria (KDIGO 2012):** Increase in creatinine ≥26.5 µmol/L (0.3 mg/dL) within 48 h, or ≥1.5× baseline within 7 days, or urine output <0.5 ml/kg/h for 6 hours (Stage 1). Staging: 1–3 based on magnitude of creatinine rise or duration of oliguria.

- **Test interpretation pattern-recognition:**

| Test | What it shows | Why order | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|-----------|----------------|
| Serum creatinine | GFR surrogate | Baseline + serial trend | Rise >26.5 µmol/L in 48 h = AKI. Doubling = Stage 2. Tripling = Stage 3 |
| Urine output | GFR (if diuretics not given) | Hourly in ICU, 4–6 h on ward | <0.5 ml/kg/h = oliguric; <0.3 ml/kg/h = severe |
| Urine dipstick | Blood, protein, leucocytes, nitrites | Screen for GN, infection, myoglobinuria | Haematuria + proteinuria → suspect GN. Blood + no RBCs → myoglobin/haemoglobin |
| Urine sediment microscopy | Casts, cells | Differentiate ATN, GN, AIN | Muddy brown granular casts = ATN; RBC casts = GN; WBC casts = AIN |
| Urine Na, creatinine, osmolality (FENa) | Tubular handling | Pre-renal vs intrinsic | FENa <1% = pre-renal; >2% = intrinsic (ATN). Note: diuretics invalidate FENa – use urea instead (FEUrea <35% pre-renal) |
| Serum K+, HCO3-, pH, Cl- | Electrolyte and acid-base | Assess hyperkalaemia, acidosis, anion gap | HyperK+ (need ECG). Metabolic acidosis with high anion gap = uraemia or lactic acidosis |
| Renal ultrasound | Hydronephrosis, kidney size, stones | Rule out obstruction (post-renal) | Hydronephrosis + ureteric dilatation = obstruction. Small kidneys (<9 cm) suggest chronic disease |
| Urine eosinophils | Eosinophiluria | Suspect AIN | Positive (but insensitive) – more useful is clinical picture + fever/rash/eosinophilia |

- **Single finding that confirms:**
  - **Pre-renal:** FENa <1% and urine osmol >500 (in the absence of diuretics)
  - **ATN:** Muddy brown granular casts + FENa >2%
  - **Post-renal:** Hydronephrosis on ultrasound
  - **GN:** Red cell casts on microscopy
  - **AIN:** WBC casts + eosinophiluria + drug exposure

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

- **Immediate (first hour)**
  - **Stop nephrotoxins:** ACEi, ARB, NSAIDs, diuretics, metformin, aminoglycosides, contrast
  - **Assess volume status:** JVP, BP, skin turgor, lung auscultation for crackles (fluid overload)
    - *If hypovolaemic:* Give 500 ml bolus of 0.9% saline over 15 min; reassess. Aim for urine output >5 ml/kg/h over the next hour. Use Hartmann’s if suspect non-anion gap acidosis.
    - *If hypervolaemic:* Restrict fluids; loop diuretic challenge (furosemide 40 mg IV; if no response, escalate).
  - **Treat hyperkalaemia (K+ >6.0 or ECG changes):**
    - Cardiac protection: 10 ml of 10% calcium gluconate IV over 2–3 min (repeats if ECG remains abnormal)
    - Shift K+ into cells: 10 units Actrapid + 50 ml 50% dextrose IV over 5 min (onset 15 min, peak 30–60 min)
    - Alternatively: 10 mg salbutamol nebulised (Caution: tachycardia, can cause hypokalaemia)
    - Remove K+: consider oral/rectal calcium resonium 15 g q8h (slow), or haemodialysis if refractory
  - **Correct acidosis:** If pH <7.2 and HCO3- <16, give 1.26% sodium bicarbonate 250 ml IV over 30 min (Caution: may precipitate hypocalcaemia).
  - **Treat precipitant:** If sepsis – start antibiotics within 1 hour; if urinary obstruction – pass a urinary catheter.

- **Ward (24–72 hours)**
  - **Monitor:** Strict fluid balance, urine output, daily creatinine, K+, Ca2+, PO4-, HCO3-, Hb, lactate
  - **Fluids:** Maintain euvolaemia. If still oliguric, consider furosemide 40–80 mg IV once (not proven to alter renal outcome but useful to avoid overload). Do not routinely give dopamine or mannitol (no benefit).
  - **Drug dose adjustment:** Use eGFR to dose all renally cleared drugs (e.g. antibiotics). Check BNF / local guidelines.
  - **Nutrition:** Restrict K+ (<2 g/day), PO4 (<800 mg/day), Na+ (<2 g/day). Ensure adequate calories (20–30 kcal/kg/day) – consider NG feeding if oral intake poor.
  - **Avoid:** IV contrast unless essential, NSAIDs, potassium-sparing diuretics, and high-protein diets.

- **Long-term**
  - **Renal replacement therapy (RRT) – indications (“AEIOU”):** A: severe acidosis (pH <7.2); E: electrolyte imbalance (K+ >6.5 refractory); I: Intoxication (e.g. lithium, methanol); O: Overload (pulmonary oedema not responsive to diuretics); U: Uraemic symptoms (encephalopathy, pericarditis, bleeding). Start haemodialysis (or CRRT in unstable patients).
  - **Follow-up:** All AKI patients need creatinine check at 3 months to assess recovery. Residual CKD is common. Avoid nephrotoxins lifelong.
  - **Education:** Patients should stay well hydrated, stop NSAIDs, and know what to do if vomiting/diarrhoea.

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | AKI (oliguric) | Pre-renal Azotaemia | ATN (Ischaemic) | AIN | Post-renal Obstruction |
|---------|----------------|---------------------|-----------------|-----|-------------------------|
| **Onset** | Rapid (hours–days) | Rapid, with volume loss | 1–3 days after insult | Days after drug start | Gradual or sudden |
| **Pain** | May have flank pain if renal capsule stretches | None | None | Flank pain ± fever | Colicky if stone; dull if chronic |
| **Urine output** | Oliguria (<0.5 ml/kg/h) | Oliguria | Oliguria (but non-oliguric possible) | Oliguria or polyuria | Anuria / polyuria (alternating) |
| **Urine sediment** | Variable; find casts | Few hyaline casts | Muddy brown granular casts | WBC casts, eosinophils (sterile pyuria) | Normal or WBCs if infection |
| **FENa** | Variable | <1% | >2% | Variable (often >1%) | Variable |
| **BUN:Cr ratio** | 10–15:1 | >20:1 (high BUN) | <15:1 | <15:1 | Variable |
| **Blood** | HyperK+, acidosis, uraemia | High BUN, Cr mildly elevated | HyperK+, acidosis, elevated Cr | Eosinophilia (if drug-induced) | Cr rises slowly, may be asymmetric |
| **Imaging** | Normal (unless chronic) | Normal | Normal | Normal or enlarged kidney | Hydronephrosis, dilated ureter, bladder distension |
| **Key differentiator** | Look for cause | Volume history + low urine Na + high BUN:Cr | Recent hypotension/toxins + FENa >2% | Drug exposure + eosinophilia + sterile pyuria | Ultrasound: hydronephrosis + post-void residual |

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

- **Hyperkalaemia (>6.5 mmol/L):** Peaked T waves, loss of P wave, wide QRS → VF/asystole. *Action:* Calcium gluconate, insulin+dextrose, bicarbonate; emergency dialysis if refractory.
- **Pulmonary oedema:** Patients become dyspnoeic, crackles up to apices, CXR shows batwing shadows; low oxygen sats. *Action:* high-flow O2, IV nitrates (GTN 10–200 µg/min), non-invasive ventilation; if no response – urgent ultrafiltration (dialysis or haemofiltration).
- **Uraemic pericarditis:** Chest pain, fever, pericardial rub, diffuse ST elevation on ECG. *Late sign:* If untreated can cause tamponade. *Action:* urgent dialysis, NSAIDs/colchicine (but risk of bleeding).
- **Uraemic encephalopathy:** Lethargy, confusion, asterixis, myoclonus, seizures, coma. *Action:* dialysis (IV thiamine if suspicion of Wernicke’s).
- **Metabolic acidosis:** Serum HCO3- <15, pH <7.30, compensatory hyperventilation (Kussmaul breathing). *Action:* correct with bicarbonate if severe; dialysis if refractory.
- **Anaemia of CKD:** Normocytic, multifactorial (erythropoietin deficiency, uraemic inhibitors). *Action:* epoetin alfa (start when Hb <100 g/L) – but not acutely.

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

- **Mnemonics:**
  - **HAS PINK** – Pre-renal causes: *H*ypovolaemia, *A*CEi/ARB, *S*epsis, *P*ancreatitis, *I*leus, *N*SAIDs, *K*idney artery stenosis.
  - **AEIOU** – Indications for dialysis: *A*cidosis (refractory), *E*lectrolyte (K+ >6.5), *I*ntoxication, *O*verload (pulmonary oedema), *U*raemic symptoms.
  - **CRASH** – Consequences of uraemia: *C*onvulsions, *R*etinitis (or asterixis), *A*cidosis, *S*erous membrane inflammation (pericarditis), *H*yperkalaemia.

- **Clinical pearls:**
  1. **BUN:Cr ratio >20:1** is highly suggestive of pre-renal azotaemia (but not reliable if patient has GI bleeding or high protein catabolism).
  2. **Urine dip positive for blood but no RBCs on microscopy = myoglobinuria** (rhabdomyolysis). Check CK; treat with aggressive IV fluids ± sodium bicarbonate.
  3. **Non-oliguric AKI (normal urine output)** is easier to manage (fluid balance) and has better prognosis. Always check creatinine even if output is normal.
  4. **Before starting diuretics in AKI, ensure the patient is euvolemic/hypervolemic.** Diuretics in hypovolaemia worsen injury.
  5. **Contrast-induced nephropathy** – risk highest in CKD, diabetes, volume depletion, high contrast volume (>300 ml). Hydrate with 0.9% saline 1 ml/kg/h for 6–12 h before and after; withhold metformin 48 h.
  6. **Sepsis is the most common cause of AKI in ICU.** Early source control, adequate fluid resuscitation (but avoid fluid overload), and antimicrobial therapy.
  7. **Always check a drug history.** Common culprits: NSAIDs, ACEi/ARB, PPIs, ciprofloxacin, rifampicin, proton pump inhibitors (AIN), and over-the-counter “herbal” remedies (e.g. Chinese herbs).

- **Common exam/ward mistakes to avoid:**
  - Not measuring urine output (must be charted).
  - Giving potassium supplements without checking K+.
  - Assuming oliguria is due to “dehydration” without assessing volume status.
  - Using urinary catheter as the first step for oliguria (can cause infection); first do bladder scan.
  - Starting dialysis too late (especially in hyperkalaemia with ECG changes).

### 10. Escalation Criteria

- **Call your registrar/senior immediately if:**
  - Creatinine rises >50% in 24 h, or >100% in 48 h (Stage 3)
  - Urine output <0.3 ml/kg/h for >6 h (Stage 3)
  - Hyperkalaemia >6.0 mmol/L not responding to initial treatment (or any ECG changes)
  - pH <7.2 (or HCO3- <12) despite bicarbonate therapy
  - Pulmonary oedema (respiratory rate >30, SpO2 <90% on 15 L O2, crackles, CXR edema)
  - GCS <12 or new neurological signs (uraemic encephalopathy)
  - NEWS2 ≥7 or lactate >4 mmol/L

- **When to discuss ICU / critical care:**
  - Any need for renal replacement therapy (dialysis)
  - Ongoing haemodynamic instability (MAP <65 despite fluids, on vasopressors)
  - Severe metabolic acidosis (pH <7.1) not correctable
  - Multiorgan failure (e.g. AKI + respiratory failure requiring ventilation)

- **When to call the crash team:**
  - Cardiac arrest (VF/asystole from hyperkalaemia)
  - Unresponsive, moribund patient with gross fluid overload and impending respiratory arrest

> **Bottom line for FY1:** A patient with AKI can deteriorate fast. Your job is to identify the cause (pre-renal, renal, post-renal), stop the trigger, treat hyperkalaemia, restore volume, and escalate early. Every hour counts.
